sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 130, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ACTION7 moved agent from rows 18-23 to rows 12-17 AND extended the trail. This is a significant gameplay change.
- Plan: Try ACTION7 again to see if it toggles. Also explore RIGHT to extend trail while at top position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 129 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 6 actions returned the board to a state already seen 6 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 6 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 5 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so ACTION7 toggles:
1. First ACTION7: agent at 18-23 → agent at 12-17, trail extended (c at rows 12-13)
2. Second ACTION7: agent at 12-17 → agent at 18-23, trail retracted (no c)
So ACTION7 toggles between:
- State A: agent at 12-17, trail extended
- State B: agent at 18-23, trail retracted
This is a toggle action. It moves the agent and extends/retracts the trail.
Now I need to think about what the goal is. The palette shows: agent - red - green - blue.
The panel shows: red - blue - green.
I've been exploring the agent's movement and the trail, but I haven't found a way to interact with the blocks.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have the agent at a specific position with the trail extended, touching the blocks in a specific order.
The trail is horizontal, at the agent's row. When the agent is at rows 12-17, the trail is at rows 12-13.
The blocks are at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green).
The trail is above all the blocks. So the trail doesn't touch any block.
But wait, the blocks move DOWN when the agent moves DOWN. So if I move the agent DOWN, the blocks move DOWN.
If I move the agent DOWN from rows 12-17 to rows 18-23, the blocks move from rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40 to rows 31-34, 37-40, 43-46.
Wait, but the agent can only move DOWN to rows 18-23. And the blocks move DOWN by 6 rows when the agent moves DOWN by 6 rows.
Actually, let me reconsider. The agent's range is rows 12-23. The agent can move between rows 12-17 and rows 18-23.
When the agent is at rows 12-17, the blocks are at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
When the agent is at rows 18-23, the blocks are at rows 31-34, 37-40, 43-46.
Wait, but earlier when the agent was at rows 18-23, the blocks were at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40. Let me re-check.
Looking at the segmentation after the second ACTION7:
- R pixels=16 rows=25-28 cols=42-45
- b pixels=16 rows=31-34 cols=42-45
- N pixels=16 rows=37-40 cols=42-45
So the blocks are at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40. But the agent is at rows 18-23.
Earlier, when the agent was at rows 12-17, the blocks were also at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
So the blocks didn't move when ACTION7 moved the agent from 12-17 to 18-23.
Wait, but earlier I observed that when the agent moves DOWN, the blocks move DOWN. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the history, when I moved the agent DOWN from rows 12-17 to rows 18-23 (2 DOWN actions), the blocks moved from rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34 to rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
So the blocks moved DOWN by 6 rows when the agent moved DOWN by 6 rows.
But now, when ACTION7 moved the agent from rows 12-17 to rows 18-23, the blocks stayed at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
So ACTION7 doesn't cause the blocks to move, even though it moves the agent DOWN.
This is interesting. ACTION7 moves the agent DOWN without moving the blocks.
Regular DOWN moves the agent DOWN and moves the blocks DOWN.
So there are two ways to move the agent DOWN:
1. Regular DOWN: moves agent and blocks DOWN
2. ACTION7: moves agent DOWN without moving blocks
And there are two ways to move the agent UP:
1. Regular UP: moves agent UP without moving blocks
2. ACTION7: moves agent UP without moving blocks (when agent is at 18-23)
Wait, but ACTION7 toggles between two states. It doesn't just move the agent UP or DOWN.
Let me re-examine:
- State A: agent at 12-17, trail extended
- State B: agent at 18-23, trail retracted
ACTION7 toggles between State A and State B.
When toggling from A to B: agent moves DOWN 6 rows, trail retracts
When toggling from B to A: agent moves UP 6 rows, trail extends
So ACTION7 is a toggle that moves the agent and changes the trail state.
Now, the key insight is that ACTION7 moves the agent without moving the blocks.
Regular DOWN moves the agent AND the blocks DOWN.
Regular UP moves the agent without moving the blocks.
So if I want to move the blocks DOWN, I need to use regular DOWN.
But the agent's range is limited to rows 12-23. When the agent is at rows 18-23, it can't move further DOWN.
Wait, but earlier when the agent was at the initial position (rows 36-41), the blocks were at rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34.
So the agent was at rows 36-41, and the blocks were at rows 19-34.
This means the agent can be at rows 36-41, which is below the blocks.
But now the agent is at rows 12-23, which is above the blocks. How did the agent get from rows 36-41 to rows 12-23?
I moved the agent UP many times. But the agent's range is only rows 12-23. So the agent can't be at rows 36-41 anymore.
Wait, maybe the agent's range changed. Or maybe the panel scrolled.
Actually, looking at the initial state, the agent was at rows 36-41. The blocks were at rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34.
After moving the agent UP, the agent is at rows 12-17. The blocks are at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
Hmm, the blocks moved DOWN when the agent moved UP? That doesn't make sense.
Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial state had the agent at rows 36-41.
After moving the agent UP many times, the agent is at rows 12-17.
But the blocks are at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40, which is different from the initial rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34.
Wait, maybe the blocks moved DOWN when the agent moved DOWN at some point. Let me trace the history.
Actually, I don't have the full history. Let me just focus on the current state and try to figure out the goal.
Current state:
- Agent at rows 18-23, trail retracted
- Blocks at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green)
- Palette: red, green, blue
The palette order is red, green, blue. The panel order is red, blue, green.
The goal might be to rearrange the blocks to match the palette order.
But how? The blocks move DOWN when the agent moves DOWN (regular DOWN).
If I move the agent DOWN, the blocks move DOWN. But the agent can only move to rows 18-23 (already there).
Wait, let me try moving the agent DOWN from rows 18-23. Earlier, the agent couldn't move further DOWN.
But maybe after ACTION7, the agent can move further DOWN.
Let me try DOWN from the current position.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: ACTION7 toggles between (agent at 12-17, trail extended) and (agent at 18-23, trail retracted). ACTION7 moves agent without moving blocks. Regular DOWN moves agent AND blocks DOWN.
Plan: Try DOWN from current position (rows 18-23) to see if agent can move further now. Also try RIGHT to extend trail.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN from current position (agent at rows 18-23)
result = action(['DOWN'])
print(f"DOWN: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 5 and n['pixels'] < 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        if r_min < 55:
            print(f"  {n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={r_min}-{r_max} cols={c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  W pixels=12 rows=19-22 cols=12-15

  G pixels=8 rows=28-31 cols=13-14
  b pixels=16 rows=31-34 cols=42-45
  G pixels=8 rows=34-37 cols=13-14
  N pixels=16 rows=37-40 cols=42-45
  g pixels=30 rows=53-53 cols=0-29
  G pixels=34 rows=53-53 cols=30-63
